---
title: "District Infant Mortality in the EAG States: An Exploratory Analysis"
subtitle: "Annual Health Survey, 2010-11 to 2012-13 (ecological, district-level)"
author: "YOUR NAME"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
library(dplyr); library(broom); library(knitr); library(here)
long  <- readRDS(here::here("outputs", "df_ahs_long.rds")) |> tibble::as_tibble()
d12   <- long |> filter(round == "2012-13") |> mutate(state = factor(state))
model <- readRDS(here::here("outputs", "imr-model.rds"))
est   <- tidy(model, conf.int = TRUE)
g     <- broom::glance(model)
getc  <- function(t) est[est$term == t, ]   # pull one coefficient row
```

## Background

Infant mortality remains high and unevenly distributed across the Empowered
Action Group (EAG) states. This exploratory, **ecological** analysis describes how
district-level infant mortality (IMR) relates to other district vital indicators
in the Annual Health Survey (AHS). Inference is about **districts, not
individuals**.

## Methods

The data are AHS district-level rates for `r nrow(d12)` districts across
`r dplyr::n_distinct(d12$state)` states, latest round (2012-13). Each row is one
district. IMR (infant deaths per 1000 live births) was modelled with **multiple
linear regression** on the crude birth rate, crude death rate, sex ratio at birth,
and state (fixed effect). The neonatal and under-five mortality rates were
**excluded as predictors** because, by definition, under-five mortality includes
infant mortality includes neonatal mortality; using them would be structural
circularity, not association. Estimates are reported with 95% confidence
intervals. Collinearity (VIF), residual normality, and influential points were
checked.

## Results

```{r}
#| label: key-numbers
n_d  <- nrow(d12)
r2   <- round(g$r.squared, 2)
imr_med <- round(median(d12$infant_mortality_rate_imr), 0)
imr_min <- round(min(d12$infant_mortality_rate_imr), 0)
imr_max <- round(max(d12$infant_mortality_rate_imr), 0)
```

Across `r n_d` districts in 2012-13, median IMR was `r imr_med` per 1000 live
births (range `r imr_min`-`r imr_max`). The model explained `r r2` of the
between-district variation in IMR (Table 1).

```{r}
#| label: tbl-model
#| tbl-cap: "Multiple linear regression: district IMR on vital indicators and state (2012-13). Estimates are mean IMR change per unit, with 95% CIs."
est |>
  mutate(term = recode(term,
    "(Intercept)" = "Intercept",
    "crude_birth_rate_cbr" = "Crude birth rate (per unit)",
    "crude_death_rate_cdr" = "Crude death rate (per unit)",
    "sex_ratio_at_birth_srb" = "Sex ratio at birth (per unit)"),
    term = sub("^state", "State: ", term)) |>
  transmute(Term = term,
            Estimate = round(estimate, 2),
            `95% CI` = sprintf("%.2f to %.2f", conf.low, conf.high),
            `p` = ifelse(p.value < 0.001, "<0.001", sprintf("%.3f", p.value))) |>
  kable()
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-dots
#| fig-cap: "District infant mortality by state, 2012-13. Each point is a district; the bar marks the state median."
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("outputs", "figures", "imr-by-state-dots-2012-13.png"))
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-trend
#| fig-cap: "Mean district IMR by state across the three AHS rounds (unweighted mean of district rates)."
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("outputs", "figures", "mean-imr-trend-by-state.png"))
```

Higher district mortality and fertility were each associated with higher infant
mortality: a one-unit higher crude death rate was associated with a
`r round(getc("crude_death_rate_cdr")$estimate, 1)`-point higher IMR
(95% CI `r round(getc("crude_death_rate_cdr")$conf.low, 1)` to
`r round(getc("crude_death_rate_cdr")$conf.high, 1)`), and a one-unit higher crude
birth rate with a `r round(getc("crude_birth_rate_cbr")$estimate, 2)`-point higher
IMR (95% CI `r round(getc("crude_birth_rate_cbr")$conf.low, 2)` to
`r round(getc("crude_birth_rate_cbr")$conf.high, 2)`). Sex ratio at birth was
**not** associated with IMR after adjustment (estimate
`r round(getc("sex_ratio_at_birth_srb")$estimate, 3)`; 95% CI
`r round(getc("sex_ratio_at_birth_srb")$conf.low, 3)` to
`r round(getc("sex_ratio_at_birth_srb")$conf.high, 3)`). Substantial differences
between states persisted after adjustment (Table 1), with Uttar Pradesh and Madhya
Pradesh districts highest relative to Bihar.

## Interpretation

These district-level patterns are **associations, not causes**. Districts with
higher general mortality and higher fertility tended to have higher infant
mortality, consistent with shared underlying determinants (poverty, health-system
access). The persistence of state effects after adjustment suggests state-level
factors not captured by these three indicators. No individual-level inference is
warranted from these aggregate rates.

## Limitations

Ecological design (district aggregates), so the ecological fallacy precludes
individual inference. The cross-sectional model uses only the 2012-13 round;
pooling rounds would require accounting for repeated measures within districts.
Residuals showed mild departure from normality (Shapiro-Wilk p = 0.03) and 12
districts were influential (Cook's D > 4/n), so estimates should be read as
descriptive. State means shown in the trend figure are **unweighted**; a
population-weighted state rate would differ. The sex-ratio convention
(females per 1000 males) is **pending confirmation** against the AHS bulletin.

## Reproducibility

```{r}
#| label: session
#| echo: true
sessioninfo::session_info()
```
